topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a stark intrusion in the otherwise calm rhythm of my evening. My breath hitched, a sudden, shallow catch in my chest. It was a request. From Mark. Mark, the one whose shadow used to stretch long and cold across the playground. My fingers hovered over the 'decline' button, trembling slightly. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room feel suddenly too bright, the sounds of the house too loud. I stared at the profile picture—a slightly distorted, overly confident grin—and felt a cold dread seep into my bones. Why now? What did he want? I quickly minimized the message, shoving my chair back with a scrape that sounded deafening in the silence.

[story 2]

I reread the message three times, my eyes darting back and forth across the screen as if the words might physically burn themselves into my vision. It was him. The phantom of my younger, more vulnerable self, now resurfacing with an unsettling familiarity. A clammy sweat prickled my palms. I physically recoiled, pulling my hand away from the keyboard as if the device itself were contaminated. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, trapped bird. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of my living room, unable to settle. Every instinct screamed at me to shut everything down, to erase the intrusion, to pretend this digital ghost didn't exist.

[story 3]

The email notification was the last thing I intended to see today. It appeared with a jarring chime, and for a full minute, I couldn't move. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my temples. I slowly leaned back in my chair, my gaze fixed on the glowing text, the letters swimming before my eyes. A distinct chill ran down my spine, a visceral tightening in my stomach that made swallowing feel like an insurmountable task. I took a deep, shaky breath, the air tasting thin. My hands began to shake uncontrollably, and I instinctively brought them up to grip the edge of the desk, anchoring myself against the sudden, dizzying sense of wrongness.
